That's awesome. There we go. Alright, this is normally when I say thanks mom, but there was more than one person clapping, so that's good. So I've presented thousands of times to audiences, but typically it's an audience of two or three or maybe five. And so I think I'm going to share some of those experiences with you today, but it's important for me to first cover how we got here. Good idea on the water, Mike. I love that. Alright, so my experience, and I'm not that much different from you, average intelligence, medium height, good enough looking to get a couple dates along the way during the course of my life, get a couple good jobs, not that much different, had some success along the way. I think what is different about my experience is that over the last ten years, I've had a different kind of selling, which is called pitching. In selling, you get to build a relationship with a customer and maybe go visit them a couple times and have some back and forth and show them your products. In my experience, I just got to walk into boardrooms and pitch one presentation, one bite of the apple, one time to a group of one, two or three people. Boom. One shot, one kill. Alright, and when you put yourself in those situations where you don't have many opportunities to come back and fix your mistakes, you think about every word and you think about every nuance and you think about every minute what needs to happen and you think about what's happening and time slows down and everything becomes relevant because you can fly a thousand miles and spend $25,000 to go to one meeting and have 20 minutes and at minute 21, it's all over. You might be in that room for an hour, but as I always say, your pitch is 20 minutes long. It's all the time you get. So very, very high stakes pitches, very high pressure circumstances. My partner, just to be specific about how this all came together, so my partner was very reckless, very wealthy guy, but reckless and he would put our money, a million dollars or two million dollars to lock up a deal. He'd say, Orrin, you have 35 days to raise $5 million. You have 42 days to raise $7 million. You have 45 days to raise $20 million and that's it. You don't do it and we lose our money. So I've lived that life for seven years and that's what gave birth to these experiences. Large deals, high stakes, high pressure consequences. What I learned is you go into a meeting and just as it says here, whether it's a phone call or you walk through the door or in some ways even on the internet, people meet and they immediately assess who has the power and who is the most needy. It's all power. You know this in your heart. It's funny in some ways I might not be perfect for this audience because I'm not motivational as you can tell. I don't care what's in your heart. Hope it's good, but I don't care. I don't care if you're motivated. I mean, I don't care if you're sad right now. My job by the end of this 45 minutes is not to make you happy. I'm not even going to try. I'm not even going to laugh because I'm a funny guy, but I'm going to motivate you. I'm not going to make you happy. I'm not going to make you sad. I don't care about all those things. The world that I function in has just three emotions, angry, hungry and horny. That's it. That's what I have to deal with so I can't think about who's happy and who's not. This is, we all kind of use that expression, eat what you kill shop and eat what you kill shop if you go to Wall Street. One of your family says I'm going to Wall Street. The first thing when they walk in the door, they're going to say you're going to eat what you kill shop. That means you make nothing unless you close, unless your pitch to close ratio is high. They're all descendants of humans that just have these emotions, angry, hungry and horny. We're going to talk a little bit about that. In my world, what I discovered, it is exhausting to absorb factual information. You know that from any time you have to take a course or seminar or get some training on a particular subject, calculus, algebra, accounting. It's very hard. At the end of 45 minutes, you're exhausted. It's hard for the mind to take factual information, but you enjoy these social seminars. You can do this all day. You can play sports even longer or do things that are fun with your family. Taking factual information is exhausting. If you walk into a room and the basic response is angry, hungry, or horny and you start giving people factual information, then you have a real problem on your hand. I had this problem for many years. You walk into a room not knowing I had 20 minutes, giving people lots of factual information over a lot of time, and my pitch is not working. I mean not selling, not closing deals. But eventually I learned to fix this. Let's go a little bit deeper into the problem. For those of you who are familiar with my material, what I discovered is these selling environments, these pitching environments are all social. They're all social. You walk into a room and you are interacting immediately with the most primitive part of the human mind, the lizard brain, the crocodile brain, primitive. That's what you get to deal with first. Then there's the midbrain, which is the social brain. Then finally, the part of the brain that you want to talk to is the neocortex that you want to communicate that can make a decision. That's the last part of the mind you get access to. This fundamental realization helped me close a half billion dollars of business. As I've said many times before, I'm not a biologist. I barely know anything about it. I didn't get through high school biology. But if you don't know this stuff, it's destructive to what you're trying to accomplish. So let's run through it quickly. You can sense this in your own mind and your body. I'll explain how now. If you think about it, you're walking through your own backyard, if you don't have one, because you live in the middle of Los Angeles or Manhattan or somebody else's backyard, and you're rustling around for a loose ball, and you see a snake. Boom! Your nervous system goes ballistic, fear, sweat. You imagine all the horrible things that are going to happen. Your nervous system takes over. It's the crock brain going, I'm afraid, fear. You got fear. But then slowly, you recognize you're in a neighborhood and an ambulance could come get you if it was necessary. An ambulance could come get you. And your social brain kicks in and goes, yes, this is a bad situation, but at least I'm around friends and family, and there's ways for me to get saved. And then your neocortex kicks in and starts aligning sight and all kinds of computational things happen. And you go, oh, crap. That's not a snake, it's a stick. And that might take five seconds. It might take three seconds to know it's a stick. But what happens is you are shaking for the next five, six, seven, ten minutes. The crocodile brain owns your nervous system, owns it. So now let's go back in the boardroom. You make somebody afraid of you, make somebody afraid of you. And the crocodile brain responds because it's the first responder because all humans care about is survival. We don't care about this stuff. Being at a conference, this was all learned and it's part of our society, but being at a conference and learning how to do the things that Paul teaches us how to do, it's amazing. By the way, never follow that guy up on stage. That guy is freaking awesome. You cannot possibly present as well as that guy. Anyway, this stuff is not what we do. We survive. That's what we're built to do. We're built to, you know, we dropped out of the trees onto the African savanna and ran eight miles a day looking for something to eat, something to kill, and someone to mate with. That's what we're made to do. Not this stuff. This stuff is a higher social order. Right? So we're creatures of survival. So you walk in a boardroom and the person you're presenting to, they're not going to listen to your information, your linguistics, and your math. That's always incredible things that you have to say. They're hyper-vigilant to fear. How can this possibly damage me? And they're focused on survival. Right? So your idea has got to come into that crocodile brain and then it's got to get into the social brain and then it gets some of the neocortex when they can make some, absorb some information which is exhausting and try and make some decisions about what you have. And this is why I said the crucial moment when it's most important to be convincing nine out of ten times we are not, or ten out of ten times for some people, because we have to understand how information flows through the other person. Let's go deeper into this subject now. Let's talk about the selling environment. So it used to be that you could offer some value to someone, something valuable. Right? You could package it up and take it in the market and who spelled customers there? Damn it. That was me. So you take it in the market and you offer to people and you get some customers and it kind of works. Right? But now today there's a huge amount of what I call white noise. Right? The signal to noise ratio of what you have is low. I want to give you a quick example. I used to work for a company called British Technology Group as a technology analyst. And they had me going to Jet Propulsion Labs in Pasadena and Caltech and Lawrence Livermore National Labs. And they said go find us great technologies at the patent level. Go find scientists and bring us. You're a hunter-gatherer for technology. Go bring us technology. So I remember one day I go to Jet Propulsion Labs and they take me 19 floors down in the basement and they introduce me to a Russian mad scientist. And I said tell me about your technology. And he says I have a low light camera that sees into space 1,000,000,000 miles further than the next guy. That's amazing. Show me this thing. It's half the size of this room. It's lenses and it's semiconductors and it goes down to the one micron and there's a specially developed blackout paint and it's amazing. This technology. I said my God you must be so proud of yourself. But isn't it lonely? Isn't it lonely having developed all this amazing technology that can see into space further than anyone else? He says it's not that bad. We have two conferences a year. One in Slovenia and one in London. I said who has this conference? Oh all the guys with low light cameras that can see billions of miles into space. Everything you are doing is that unique in these most esoteric unbelievably sophisticated technologies that take mad scientists their whole careers to develop and there's 40 other guys that do it and they get together twice a year to drink beer. It is not possible that you sell protein powder on the internet and there's not 20, 30, 50, 1,000 other guys doing it. There's a huge amount of noise. Everybody's, there's many, many people clamoring for the same space. What do I see happening? Especially on the internet but certainly in pitching and selling. If there's lots of white noise then we turn up the signal. Probably half the people in this room including myself have done that. Writing in all caps. Writing amazing and excellent in all caps. What is that? It's turning up the signal. The volume because you're trying to break through. I got this great product but nobody will listen to me. So I got to turn up the volume. All caps. Exclamation points. Oh let's highlight it in yellow. Let's circle it in red. Turn up the volume. Amplify everything. And now we do break through. The signal to noise ratio goes higher. We break through the white noise when we turn up the volume. But what happens also? We get people's attention but we start to create fear because we've got a signal that's so much higher than people are expecting to see. So when you sell so aggressively you do, you do get people's attention and you do break through the white noise but you also trigger in the crocodile brain you trigger fear. I'll just go back to that. Because this is where everything comes up through. So when we raise the volume so aggressively we trigger fear. So then what happens is people go to conversion funnels. We do it ourselves and you have to do it. But conversion funnel goes more into analytics and it goes further away from the social environment. And here's my conclusion. So when you're selling something or you're pitching a deal, you don't have an option for this. Your ideas, your concepts, the things you want to say come up through the crocodile brain. You have to just do that in a way that's safe. You can't screw that up. So you've got to watch how much volume you're applying to not create fear. We can't deliver information right away because information is exhausting to consume. You burn people out and the minute you start delivering information the clock starts ticking. You may not have enough time. So to me it's all social. It all happens in this midbrain area. This midbrain developed because as we moved from individuals seeking to eat something, kill something, mate from something, we moved into tribes and we moved into communities. And this part of the brain developed to track large communities within our own tribe and other tribes. And it's very, very powerful. I'll just say this one thing quickly. About a three-year-old doesn't know algebra, doesn't know calculus, doesn't know Shakespeare. I mean maybe some of the people in this room look damn smart and their three-year-olds can do all that stuff. But the three-year-olds in my family just can't do that. They barely walk. But a three-year-old knows very, very broad social environments. They know who's a policeman, they know who's a teacher, they know who's an aunt and an uncle and who's a brother and who's a mom and who can deliver the power stroke and who they can control. They don't know anything about reading and Shakespeare and linguistics and math, but they have very, very broad understanding of social environments. So you can see it happening. You see a stick that you think is a snake. It takes over your nervous system. You see a three-year-old, they understand the difference between a cop and a teacher and a mom and a neighbor and somebody they'd never met. Very, very broad social environments. And finally, you can know in your own mind when you have to listen to information, it is very exhausting. So I believe it all boils down to what can you do in the social environment. So I think today the problem is we deliver information to someone, right, and then they begin to reject us. Maybe if some people, small percentages, say I love it and I want to buy, we deliver a value proposition and then they begin to reject us. This is why I say selling today or pitching is based on rejection control. Rejection control, which is, oh, you are objecting to my sale. Let me overcome your objection. Well, we don't do that at all. Oh, I could definitely hear you guys back there. Okay. Note to self, Mike. All right. All right. Overcoming objections has become the thing that we do, right? So we give some information, buy my product, invest in my deal, do what I say. And then we get an objection back. And then we try and overcome that objection. It's rejection control. And I watch most of my contemporaries try and sell something. And what happens? They deliver information, deliver information to the buyer. Then the buyer asks a question. Then they answer the question. And then the buyer asks another question. They answer it. And it's like a game of Gnip Gnop or tennis. I just see it going back and forth. There's no social control in there. Nothing is happening. It's just information moving back and forth. It's not good. Right. So rejection control, unlike traditional sales methods, are predictable. And they're easy to defeat. They take no training. So in my experience, you walk into a room, you start to deliver information to somebody to buy your deal, right? We're selling a company. It's $15 million. We'd like you to pay $15 million for it. Here's the information. Well, how do you guys account for the fact that there's not good transparency and visibility in the next three quarters of revenue? Oh, let me tell you how we account for that. Well, what about the product? Aren't there competitors? Oh, here's a competitive landscape. Well, the management team is the management team. Any good? I'm looking at their bios. It doesn't look like they've done this before. Oh, let me tell you about the management team. Right. That's all rejection control. So what I've learned is it doesn't work very well. So now let's move forward to what I think does work. Let me say one more thing. Economic transactions are about power, right? You walk into a room and the guy with the money has the power. The guy with the money has the power. He's got, you know, we immediately assess, as I said before, who's the purchasing power? Who is the information power? Who has the staying power to last longest? These aren't my terms. You guys are all familiar with it. Oh, they have good purchasing power. Who has staying power? Who has the most options? The guy with the most options has the most power, and that's always the money, right? So you walk into a room and you immediately get the money. Bump into this power frame. Well, we have the money, so you pitch me your product, because let me listen to you. I will be the judge of what you have, and I'll be the judge of what you have. Sell me something because I have money and I have the power. Like in Star Wars, Senator Palpatine. Power, right? Power. Who has the power? You walk in a room, the guy with the money has the power, and that's a real problem because it's not fun to play power games. So when somebody is the king and they can judge you, they have the power, and you're sitting there. So that makes you the supplicant. Supplicant, as I've said before, is a person who's trying to prove that they're worth the purchase order or getting the money or getting approval. But that's the buyer-seller relationship. That's the buyer-seller relationship. The other guy has the money, he has the power, and we are trying to sell a product, so we have to supplicate to him. So the power frame always tries to get economic concessions from you in exchange for intangibles. That's what power does, right? So the bigger the boardroom, you know, I had a picture two months ago at two in Barcadero. You think this is nerve-racking sitting in front of 500 people? I mean, it's only 10 people. Two in Barcadero, overlooking the entire San Francisco Bay, a conference room table that goes from here to the back of the room. It's like a hockey rink. Art work on the wall worth more than I've made in my entire life. Right? Billionaires sitting there. It's power, right? And you get a sense, like, I want to give a concession to these guys just to be on their playing field. Think of everything they could do for me. If I'm in business with them, they can introduce me to other guys I want to know. I'm going to give them a good price. Because all the power they have, and they use it, right? They want to get economic concessions. They want me to lower my price and give me nothing. And I do it because it's the power frame. So let's talk about ways to deal with this. Go back to the brain. Whenever then we encounter the power frame, we start to sell hard, right? Because we get a sense, oh, we're going to have to give an economic concession. Let me give, like, a little bit of effort before I concede. We start to sell hard, right? Then what happens? The primordial response of their crock brain kicks in and they become afraid of us. And their power goes up, our status goes lower, and we have a harder time. We've created fear. We're not in a social environment. And we're nowhere near. Nowhere near being able to pitch the neocortex. It's a horrible situation. The crock brain is a gatekeeper. We got to get past it, right? So let's, I think, that makes the problem deeply felt. You walk into a room, the other guys have the power. We start to supplicate. They start to use their power. So we start to sell harder. There's lots of white noise. We feel like our signal to noise ratio is low. And we start to pump up the volume on our messaging. You pump up the volume of the messaging. The defensive mechanisms of the crock brain kick in. We're not working in a social environment, which the mind responds to. And now we have all kinds of selling and pitching and presenting problems. And things start going cattywampus. Start going cattywampus. It's not good. So if you look at this, you start to say, I need to solve this. And for me, if you believe you have to take care, you have to take care of the crock brain. And you can't do much better to organize your information than it's all social. So what tools do we have to work in a social environment? So we just want to, this is the last time we'll visit the brain, right? So when you have to take, when I say you have to take care of the crock brain, I mean something very specific. You go in, you get somebody on the phone, you walk into a room, you get them on an internet site. You have to take care of the crock brain so it doesn't run away from you. That means you have to do things fast. You can't wax theoretical. You have to convey information that's meaningful and do it fast. And it has to be novel. Nobody comes to a damn meeting to hear about things they already know, from people they already know, about outcomes they can guess. They only come to a meeting or a conference or fly a thousand miles and pay money to do something because they're going to learn something new and see colorful people and hear about interesting things and go to exotic places and experience new experiences. That's the only reason people will come. The only reason people will come to a phone call or a meeting or an internet page for novelty, have to provide it. And it has to be concrete. The second you're vague and fuzzy, you know this crocodile mind goes, you know, I'm not afraid of it. It's boring and it's too complicated. I'm out of here and do some things related to survival. This doesn't affect me. So it's got to be concrete. It has to be visual. This goes 50 times faster than auditory or sensory. So if you really want access to the mind, you go visual. Imagine driving to work at one mile an hour. You can get there very fast for comparison sake. It's got to be visual, and it's got to be high contrast. This crock morning doesn't understand things that are similar to each other. visual high contrast. So now we enter what I believe. You walk into a room, you enter the power frame, right? And if that, if there's such a thing as a power frame, then what do you want? You want frame control. This is what I've said many times and hopefully this is maybe the second or third or fourth time you're hearing this. But let me tell you what my definition of a frame is. Definition of a frame. It's interesting when I was writing the book, I thought it was going to be easy to define frames. I bought 50, 60 books from psychologists from Columbia and MIT and Harvard, Yale, all about frames and frame control. And none of them have good definitions that were usable. So here's my definition. Each of us have a frame. We come to a meeting and we bring a frame with us. That's our point of view. It's our perspective. It's our charisma. It's all the information we have, right? So I think my deal is worth a million, sorry, I think my deal is worth say five million dollars. Right? And you bring your frame and you think the deal is worth one million dollars. And these frames come together in a meeting. You can watch them in slow motion. Right? Five million and one million are incompatible points of view. If a frame is a point of view, then these frames collide and they crash into each other and they bend and crumple like a car accident and they smash each other. And eventually one frame is always the stronger frame and it crushes and breaks the weaker frame and absorbs it. And you know this because in any meeting you've ever been in where there is an outcome, there is one stronger perspective, one stronger point of view, one stronger frame and that frame dominates. So we're always seeking frame control where our point of view is the dominant point of view. Now it doesn't mean we're alpha males even if we're females exhibiting alpha behavior, right? Aggressive and dominant. That's not what we're looking for. We're looking for a dominant frame and we can do it in most relaxed and poised and simple and easy and calm way. We don't have to be at battle stations to get frame control, but what we do need is some tools to get it, right? It's not about elevating voice and aggression and being the alpha animal. So in a social environment we avoid crock brain problems and then we look for tools to get frame control, right? And some of those tools are to elevate your status because people fall into the frame of people of high status, right? Status comes from celebrity, influence and money. Status comes so when you have a celebrity Brad Pitt or Morgan Freeman you defer to him. He wants to go to this restaurant, you want to go somewhere else, you go to where he wants to go, he's a celebrity, right? The commander of the armed forces wants to go to a restaurant, you go with him. He's got influence, you're not going to go against him. And Donald Trump wants to go to restaurant, he's got very high status because he has money. So status comes from money, comes from influence and it comes from celebrity. So you want to create some status for yourself. You can control these power frames with time frames, right? Time frames control outcomes, right? So if you all guys all know this, if you go to a meeting, you say wait five minutes, you know that meeting is all about the delivery of information because there's not going to be any social relationship. But if you say it's a two hour meeting, then there's going to be all kinds of social interaction. So time frames are way of controlling power. We talk about how to do this specifically. Let's talk about prizing. So this is something that I developed to overcome the power frame. So what I realized is the power frame is mainly built on money. Now we're going to do some practical tools. The power frame, you walk in a room, they have the money, you don't, you're asking for a purchase order, you're asking for them to write you a check, they're asking to buy what you have, right? And you find yourself in the supplicant. So when they say, you know, we like what you have, but why don't you fetch me a blue rock? Because I need a little bit more information, you go fetch a blue rock, right, to, to supplicate to the power frame. They say I love the blue rock, right? Go fetch me a green rock now and you go get it some information. They say, again, oh, I love this green rock. I'm ready to buy. But now we need a purple rock and you run and you go to get a purple rock. We supplicate to the power frame. So how do we take the power frame away? And for me, now we're going to enter social tools of framing. And I thought about this and I tried many, many things and I lost many, many deals, right, to the point where my partners were getting frustrated with me. You don't know what you're doing. You don't know how to sell. You don't know how to deliver information. You don't know how to answer questions. You don't know how to overcome objections. I don't believe that rejection control would work. I believe social control, frame control would work. So eventually I came up with this concept of pricing, where you just take the money away. Okay, Mr. Buyer, you're not the judge of me because all you have is money and I don't care about money. Money is a commodity. I can get it from an ATM machine. I can go get a loan. I can spend less. Money is a commodity. It's more of a commodity than rice. It's more of a commodity than oil. Money is a commodity. You do not get to make the decisions. You do not get to tell me to go fetch another rock. You don't get to control this agenda because you have money because I don't care about money. Money is a commodity and it's available anywhere. Nobody has what I have. The blue plastic frogs that I have are the baddest in the world. They kill insects deader over more coverage, faster and they're cute. Look here, you bastard. I'm in the business of having them and you're in the business of wanting them. So there's some confusion in this room and nobody's fetching a rock. Right. And the practical way you do it is
